text: Today we present a case of superior mesenteric artery syndrome four months following sleeve gastrectomy. There are no disclosures. The patient is a thirty-nine-year-old female who underwent laparoscopic sleeve gastrectomy for a BMI of thirty-six. She initially did well with rapid weight loss, then developed unrelenting nausea, emesis, and epigastric pain. At the time of consultation four months post-operatively, she had lost seventy pounds to a BMI of twenty-four.

CT scan demonstrated significant narrowing of the duodenum between the aorta and SMA with dilation of the duodenum proximal to this, suspicious for SMA syndrome. Duplex sonography demonstrated a decreased angle between the aorta and SMA to eighteen degrees and a distance of five point eight millimeters, diagnostic for SMA syndrome. SMA syndrome is obstruction of the duodenum due to compression between the aorta and SMA. This is often precipitated by extreme weight loss. Symptoms are nonspecific but have been well described in the literature with postprandial fullness, nausea, epigastric pain, and emesis. Symptoms improve with sitting forward or in the left lateral decubitus position.

The diagnostic criteria by duplex or CT require an aortomesenteric angle less than twenty-five and distance less than ten millimeters, both of which were present in this patient. SMA syndrome is extremely rare, occurring in less than one percent of the general population. In the bariatric population, the incidence is based only on case reports. Surgical options include gastrojejunostomy, but Strong's procedure and duodenal jejunostomy are best described. Strong's procedure involves division of the ligament of trites with duodenal mobilization and caudal displacement of the duodenal jejunal flexure. Duodenal jejunostomy is also well described and has a success rate of seventy-nine to one hundred percent. The first laparoscopic case was described in nineteen ninety-eight.

In the operating room, the surgeon performs a laparoscopic duodenal jejunostomy. Endoscopy reveals a normal gastric sleeve and significant narrowing of the duodenum at the point where the superior mesenteric artery (SMA) crosses it. The transverse colon is reflected to identify the middle colic vessels, duodenum, and SMA. The peritoneal lining of the duodenum is incised, and the duodenum is mobilized distally and proximally. A jejunal limb, approximately thirty centimeters distal to the ligament of Treitz, is prepared for anastomosis, with a stay suture placed to orient the anastomosis.

The surgeon creates enterotomies in the duodenum and jejunum, forming an anastomosis using a linear stapler while carefully avoiding damage to the superior mesenteric artery at the distal tip. A stay suture is placed posteriorly to orient the common enterotomy, and the defect in the anastomosis is over-sown in two layers with running Vicryl suture.
The endoscope is reinserted to inspect the anastomosis intraluminally, and a leak test is performed using methylene blue. The anastomosis appears intact, with no visible leaks.

SMA syndrome is a rare diagnosis, but should be considered after extreme weight loss, especially in the bariatric population. Laparoscopic duodenal jejunostomy is a technically easy and successful option for the management of SMA syndrome.
